title: Setting Boundaries and Expectations Before the Move
summary: Clear, spoken-out-loud expectations prevent most of the friction that comes with multigenerational living.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Setting Boundaries and Expectations Before the Move | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Clear, spoken-out-loud expectations prevent most of the friction that comes with multigenerational living.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Household rules that were fine unstated before now need to be said explicitly.
Financial contributions (or lack thereof) are worth discussing directly and early.
Privacy and "alone time" needs apply to every generation in the house, not just the parent moving in.

paragraphs: Things that felt obvious in a two-person household — quiet hours, guest policies, how shared spaces get used — often need to be spoken aloud once a parent moves in, since assumptions rarely match perfectly across generations.

Money is one of the most commonly avoided topics in this conversation, and one of the most important to address directly and early — whether that means a financial contribution, covering specific costs, or an explicit agreement that no contribution is expected.

nextSteps: Write out household expectations together rather than assuming they're understood.
Address financial contributions directly, even if the answer is "none needed".
Revisit the agreement periodically as the arrangement evolves.
disclaimer: General guidance for a conversation every family navigates differently.

sources.name: Home Away from Home: Relocating Your Parents
